title: pinopsida
intro: The rank Pinopsida refers to a division of coniferous plants that includes species such as pine, spruce, and fir trees, characterized by needle-like leaves and cone-like reproductive structures. Pinopsida plants are typically evergreen, meaning they retain their leaves year-round, and often have adapted to survive in harsh, temperate environments. This division is distinct from others such as Commelinidae, which includes plants like bamboo and palms, and Rosidae, which encompasses a wide range of flowering plants including roses and apples. In contrast, Asteridae is a division that includes plants like sunflowers and daisies, all of which are characterized by their showy, flower-like reproductive structures.

subclasses.subclass_desc: Pinidae: a subclass of coniferous plants within the class Pinopsida, characterized by needle-like leaves and cones that produce seeds.
Gnetidae is a small and ancient subclass of gymnosperms that includes conifers, cycads, and gnetophytes, characterized by their unique and diverse reproductive structures.
Coniferae is a subclass of gymnosperms characterized by cone-bearing, evergreen trees and shrubs.
Ginkgoidae is a subclass of the taxonomical classification of plants that includes the single living species Ginkgo biloba and its extinct relatives.
Austrobaileyanae is a subclass of gymnosperms within the taxonomic classification of plants, characterized by seed-producing plants with vascular tissues.
